poem.content:
The early rose, a blush against the grey,
Heard the ocean talk, learned what the tides convey.
About the law, the shifting, hungry sand,
And how the fury claws across the land.

A company of shadows, drawing near,
Behind each mask, a chilling, silent fear.
We depend on whispers, carried on the breeze,
Among the lemon groves and bending trees.

To laugh is to invite a cruel test,
To show the happy heart within your breast.
But smiles are fragile, easily undone,
This world can damage all beneath the sun.

He pulled the mask, revealing what remained,
A face the fury of the ocean stained.
No rose survives the bitterness of brine,
No lemon brightens what the tides define.

He learned the law: existence is the fight,
Against the crushing waves and endless night.
And yet, the tide will turn, they say,
Washing the damage into yesterday.
